concerns.text: The plan proposes using a 'mass-spring model' to approximate VDOS if phonon calculations are too heavy. This introduces a severe construct validity risk: a harmonic mass-spring model on a static graph cannot capture the anharmonic phonon-phonon scattering that dominates thermal conductivity in amorphous silicon. Using this proxy as a predictor for thermal conductivity may yield spurious correlations or fail to detect the true signal, invalidating the test of H-001/H-002.

The plan acknowledges the study is observational but proposes testing H-001 (correlation > 0.7) and H-002 (R² > 0.1) as if they are definitive validations. Without a mechanism to control for the confounding variable of 'local density' or 'coordination number' (which drives both ring statistics and thermal conductivity), any observed correlation may be a proxy for density rather than a specific topological effect. The plan lacks a multivariate control strategy (e.g., partial correlation or including density as a covariate) to isolate the topological contribution.

The plan assumes a sample size N < 100 configurations. For a Random Forest model with ~10-15 topological/vibrational features, this sample size is statistically underpowered to reliably estimate feature importance or non-linear interactions without severe overfitting, even with LOOCV. The plan does not address how to validate model stability or generalizability given this high feature-to-sample ratio.
